content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
November 29, 1990


Mr. Emery B. Dowell
P. O. Box 191167
Sacramento, CA  95819

                                    Re:  Your Request for Informal
                                         Assistance
                                         Our File No. I-90-656

Dear Mr. Dowell:

     This is in response to your written request for informal
advice concerning your responsibilities under the conflict of
interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").
Because you have specifically requested informal advice and you
are not facing a specific governmental decision, we provide you
with informal advice.  (Regulation 18329(c), copy enclosed.)²  Our
advice is limited to questions arising under the Act.³

                              QUESTION

     To what extent do you, as a member of the Major Medical
Insurance Board (the "Insurance Board") and as a director of Blue
Cross of California ("Blue Cross"), face a disqualifying conflict
of interest in carrying out your duties on the Insurance Board?

                              CONCLUSION

     Because Blue Cross has been a source of income to you in the
last 12 months, you may not participate in any governmental
decision in which Blue Cross is directly involved.  You may also
have a disqualifying conflict of interest if there is a "nexus"
between the decision and the purpose for which you receive income.


_____________________________________________________________________

     ¹ Government Code Sections 81000-91015.  All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000, et seq.  All references to regulations are to
Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

     ² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section
83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)

     ³ We offer no advice concerning Government Code Section 1090.  If
you have questions regarding application of Section 1090, you
should contact the Attorney General's office.

428 J Street, Suite 800  •  P.O. Box 807  •  Sacramento CA 95804-0807  •  (916) 322-5660

Our File No. I-90-656
Page 2

from Blue Cross, or if it is reasonably foreseeable that the
decision will have a material financial effect on Blue Cross, even
if Blue Cross is only indirectly involved in the decision.

FACTS

You have been appointed to serve as a member of the Major
Medical Insurance Board, which will be implementing the California
Major Medical Insurance Program. (Insurance Code Sections 12700,
et seq. The Board's duties include determining what insurance
coverage to provide, and deciding which insurance carriers should
receive contracts to provide the coverage. (Id., section 12710.)
According to your letter, the program is designed so the insurance
carriers chosen by the Board will receive specific premiums, will
be reimbursed for medical costs that exceed the premiums received,
and will be paid a specific amount to cover costs of
administration and claims payments. Your letter goes on to say,
however, that the contracts with the carriers will be designed to
prevent any profit or loss to the carriers.⁴

During our November 9 phone conversation with your office, we
were informed that the Insurance Board will soon develop a
"prototype" contract, the final version of which will be the
contract under which insurance carriers will operate pursuant to
the program.

You have been a director of Blue Cross for three years and
your present term is for two more years. Blue Cross, according to
your letter, is a 501(c)(6) nonprofit entity in the health
insurance business. Your letter does not expressly say so, but
the implication is that the Insurance Board is considering or will
consider awarding a contract to Blue Cross to effectuate the Major
Medical Insurance Program.

You receive $12,000 per year as compensation for your
directorship with Blue Cross. You are also paid retirement
benefits by Blue Cross, though it is not clear from your letter
whether they are paid because of your directorship.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 mandates that "no public official at any level
of state or local government shall make, participate in making or
in any way attempt to use his or her official position to
influence a governmental decision in which the official knows or
has reason to know he or she has a financial interest."⁵

_______________
⁴ We have made no determination whether the program will in fact
prevent profit or loss to Blue Cross or any other carrier.

⁵ In addition to Section 87100, you should be aware of Section
84308. It discusses the limitations on appointed board members,
such as you, to participate in a decision if the member has
accepted, solicited or directed political contributions from a
party or participant in the case.

Our File No. I-90-656
Page 3


Thus, to answer your question, it is necessary to ask: (1)
Are you a public official under Section 87100? (2) What
constitutes making or influencing a governmental decision? (3)
Do you have a financial interest based on your position at Blue
Cross?

I. Public Official

A "public official" is every natural person who is a member
of a state or local governmental agency. (Regulation 18700(a),
copy enclosed.) "Member" includes unsalaried members of boards
with decision-making authority. (Regulation 18700(a)(2).) "State
agency" includes every state office, department, division, bureau,
board and commission. (Section 82049.) The Insurance Board is
part of the Business, Transportation and Housing Agency (Insurance
Code Section 12710) and its decision-making authority is
established by Insurance Code Section 12711. Accordingly, you are
a public official under Section 87100 because you are a member of
a board that is part of a state agency with decision-making
authority.

II. Governmental Decisions

A public official makes a "governmental decision" if, when
acting within his or her official authority, the official votes on
a matter, appoints a person, obligates or commits his or her board
or commission to a course of action or enters into a contract on
its behalf. (Regulation 18700(b).) By deciding not to do one of
these things, the official would also be making a governmental
decision, unless the decision was based on a disqualifying
financial interest and it is disclosed.6

Under certain circumstances, a public official "participates
in the making of a governmental decision" by negotiating with a
governmental entity, private person or business entity regarding a
decision, or by giving advice or recommendations to the decision
maker. (Regulation. 18700(c)(2).)

A public official "attempts to influence a governmental
decision" if, for purposes of influencing the decision, he or she
contacts, appears before, or otherwise attempts to influence any
member, officer, employee or consultant of his or her agency.
(Regulation 18700.1(a) copy enclosed.) Therefore, you would be
making a governmental decision at the Insurance Board if you were
to try to influence any of your fellow board members.

III. Economic Interest

A public official has a financial interest in a governmental
decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will
have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect


_______________________
6 Specific disclosure requirements are set forth in Regulation
18700(b)(5).

Our File No. I-90-656
Page 4

on the public generally, on the official or on a member of his or
her immediate family, or on a source of income to the official if
the source of income pays or promises to pay income totalling $250
or more, within 12 months prior to the time when the governmental
decision is made. (Section 87103(c).)

From the facts you provided, it appears that Blue Cross is a
source of income to you of over $250 within the last 12 months.
Therefore, if it is foreseeable that a decision will materially
affect Blue Cross, you must disqualify yourself.

IV. Foreseeability

The effects of a decision are reasonably foreseeable if there
is a substantial likelihood that they will occur. To be
foreseeable, the effects of a decision must be more than a mere
possibility, though certainty is not required. (Downey Cares v.
Downey Development Com. (1987) 196 Cal.App.3d 817, 983, 989-991;
Witt v. Morrow (1977) 70 Cal.App.3d 817, 822; In re Thorner (1975)
1 FPPC Ops. 198.)

V. Materiality

Whether a decision materially affects a source of income
turns on whether the economic interest is directly or indirectly
involved in the decision. A source of income is directly involved
in a decision if it initiates the proceeding in which the decision
will be made by filing a claim, application, appeal or similar
request, is a named party in or subject of the proceeding,7 or if
a "nexus" exists between the purpose for which the official
receives income and the governmental decision. There is a nexus
between the purpose for which an official receives income and a
governmental decision if the official receives income to achieve a
goal or purpose that would be achieved, defeated, aided or
hindered by the decision. (Regulation 18702.1(d).)

If there is a nexus or the source of income is directly
involved, the effect of a decision is material. (Regulation
18702.1(a)(1).) Thus, you correctly determined that under the Act
you may not participate in any decision that directly involves
Blue Cross because Blue Cross has been a source of $250 or more in
income to you in the last 12 months.

Even if Blue Cross is not a party to or does not initiate the
proceeding in which the decision will be made, you must determine
whether there is a nexus between the purpose for which you receive
the income from Blue Cross and the decision. For example, if the
purpose for which Blue Cross pays you to serve as a director would

7
A person or business entity is the subject of a proceeding if
a decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial or
revocation of any licenses, permit, or other entitlement to, or
contract with the subject business entity. (Regulation
18702.1(d).)

Our File No. I-90-656
Page 5

be achieved, defeated, aided, or hindered by a decision before the
Insurance Board, a nexus would exists and thus the financial
effect would be material. (Regulation 18702(d).) Presumably,
Blue Cross pays you for serving as a director in order to promote
its interests, manage its affairs, fulfill its legal obligations
as a corporation, promote its business or otherwise help it conduct
its business. You have not provided us with enough facts
regarding decisions before the Board to determine whether a nexus
exists. It is possible, however, that decisions concerning, for
example, the prototype contract will achieve, defeat, aid or
hinder the reasons for which Blue Cross pays you to serve as a
director.

If the decision only involves Blue Cross indirectly, whether
the effect of the decision is material will be determined by
Regulation 18701.5, upon your characterization of Blue Cross as a
nonprofit entity.

The effect of a decision may be material as to a nonprofit
entity that is a source of income to a public official, depending
on the amount of the entity's gross annual receipts and the effect
the governmental decision will have on the entity's gross receipts
and its expenses. (Regulation 18702.5(a).) Thus, for example, if
Blue Cross' annual receipts are $400 million or more, the effect
of an Insurance Board decision will be material if it results in
an increase or decrease in Blue Cross' gross annual receipts for a
fiscal year in the amount of $1 million or more, or if the
decision will cause Blue Cross to incur or avoid additional
expenses or to reduce or eliminate existing expenses for a fiscal
year in the amount of $250,000 or more, or if the decision will
result in an increase or decrease in the value of Blue Cross'
assets or liabilities in the amount of $1 million or more.
(Ibid.)

I trust this letter responds to your inquiry. Should you
have any further questions regarding this matter, please do not
hesitate to call me at (916) 322-5901.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel

By: Steven R. Rhoads
Counsel, Legal Division

SH:SRR:dg

Enclosures
